lever_name: High-skill immigration fast-track and STEM-prioritized permanent-residency pathway
kpi: Net Migration Flow of AI Skills; Tortoise Talent Score
effect_low: Canadian citizens pursuing U.S. labour certification fell 26% from 3,310 (2015) to 2,460 (2024)
effect_base: Canada saw a 29% one-year increase in AI professionals in 2022–2023
effect_high: Canada’s AI talent cohort has grown by an average 38% per year since 2018
source: Global Skills Strategy 2-week processing standard; Express Entry category-based selection; CIFAR AI impact reporting; Statistics Canada; CIFAR
rationale: Best available Canadian talent anchors are ecosystem-level outcomes and migration proxies, not clean policy elasticities for AI workers alone.

lever_name: Transition-to-PR retention pipeline for skilled entrants
kpi: Net Migration Flow of AI Skills; Tortoise Talent Score
effect_low: Former work-permit holders had 78% five-year provincial retention after transition to PR
effect_base: Former work-permit holders had 91% first-year retention, falling to 78% by year five
effect_high: Ontario’s five-year retention rate for immigrants admitted in 2017 reached 93.5%
rationale: Not AI-specific, but this is the cleanest federal administrative evidence for converting skilled temporary entrants into long-stay workers.

kpi: AI Investment Activity; Tortoise Commercial Score
effect_low: ISED-supported firms posted 17% revenue growth in 2023–2024 versus 4% nationally
effect_base: ISED-supported firms posted 24.9% revenue growth in 2022–2023 versus 12.4% nationally
effect_high: NRC IRAP IP Assist clients reported revenue growth 42% versus 33% for other NRC IRAP clients at first access
source: ISED Departmental Results Reports; NRC IRAP IP Assist evaluation
rationale: Best Canadian commercialization anchors are firm-growth differentials from federal programs; they are useful for calibration, but not AI-only causal estimates.

lever_name: R&D tax incentives / direct funding tied to commercialization-ready firms
kpi: AI Patent Grants (per 100k); Tortoise Development Score
effect_low: Canada direct government funding showed 0.078 ATT on patents
effect_base: Canada R&D tax incentives showed 0.130 ATT on patents
effect_high: Canada R&D tax incentives showed 0.229 ATT on intramural R&D; direct funding showed 0.208 ATT on intramural R&D
source: OECD microBeRD Canada estimates; NRC IRAP IP Assist lag note on patent timing
rationale: This is one of the few Canada-specific causal evidence bases available for innovation output, but it is not AI-specific and maps more cleanly to patents/R&D than to immediate commercial score jumps.

lever_name: Refundable adoption subsidy / voucher for first deployment
effect_low: SMEs receiving government support were 8% more likely to increase digitalisation
effect_base: SMEs receiving more than one type of government support had digitalisation levels up to 3x greater
effect_high: Not directly estimated for an AI-specific Canadian tax credit
source: OECD SME digitalisation evidence; Canada AI adoption baseline; Singapore 100E timing anchor
rationale: The strongest quantitative analog is broader digital-adoption support, not a direct AI-tax-credit impact study. Use this for AI Adoption KPI calibration, with wide uncertainty bands.
confidence: medium on direction, weak on size

kpi: Tortoise Commercial Score; AI Investment Activity
effect_low: A 10% rise in past government contract obligations is associated with a 0.2% rise in firm R&D expenses the following year
effect_base: Not directly estimated for private-sector first-customer credits
source: OECD public procurement and innovation study
rationale: The closest authoritative analog to first-customer support is procurement-led innovation. It supports directionality and lag, but not a clean private-enterprise tax-credit elasticity.
